Proposal · Library Lobby · MAHE Manipal · July 2026

The Wall is a Letter

Lipi — a cipher script for the library
Ujjwal Agarwal — in response to the note on Journeys

A long wall in the lobby, covered end to end in a script no one has ever seen. It is beautiful, and it is patient: it is not a pattern. Every mark is a letter, every cluster a word — the wall carries a real text, waiting for the reader willing to walk its length and decode it.

The script is designed, not borrowed — an alphabet of thirty-six letterforms (A–Z, 0–9) built by a generative type system I have written for this work. Each letter is assembled from a shared anatomy of spines, bowls, arms and dots, under one set of proportions, so the alphabet reads as a single coherent hand: something that could have been excavated, though it belongs to no place and no language. It carries the posture of script itself — and nothing else.

The wall

Script at wall length

This panel is drawn live by the working engine, letter by letter — what you see is the actual system, not an illustration. Press reissue and it designs an entirely new alphabet on the spot: new letterforms, new proportions, same hidden text. The final wall commits to one script, chosen the way one chooses a typeface — slowly.

The key

Anyone can read it — slowly

Near the wall hangs a small engraved plate: the glossary. Thirty-six glyphs, each with its plain letter beneath. That is the entire mechanism. A passer-by sees abstract beauty; a curious reader stops, borrows the key, and begins — letter by letter, word by word, walking back and forth along the wall. Decoding is deliberately slow reading: the text cannot be skimmed, photographed into sense, or finished in a visit. The library’s lobby quietly teaches its oldest lesson — that reading is an act of patience — disguised as a game.

The text itself is the committee’s to choose, and that is part of this proposal: a passage on learning from the Vedas, a poem, a line each from the disciplines the library serves — the wall can hold roughly a thousand words at reading height. Until it is decoded, it is pure form; after, it is a message a reader has earned. Both readings are correct.

The demonstration above encodes a line from the commissioning note itself: “Reading extends beyond the page. Learning unfolds through materials, movement, conversation and imagination.”
the first decoder will find out
Slow AttentionScript Open-ended InterpretationLeela / Play Vidya / AvidyaBody / Movement Tactile / Material
In the space

Views of the work

Form
One long wall (or a series of panels along the lobby), drawn in ink or paint at 60–120 mm letter height; the glossary as a small engraved or plotted plate nearby
Making
Pen-plotted panels (a 3 m panel is about twenty minutes of machine drawing), or lasercut stencils and paint for permanent walls — both from the same files
Maintenance
None. It is ink on a wall. Nothing runs, nothing listens, nothing updates
Reproducible
The entire script regenerates exactly from a single seed number — the library can catalogue its own alphabet
Pilot
One A1 panel and its glossary plate, plotted and framed, within two weeks of a yes
lipi · the wall is a letter · decode it slowly
